text.title: Gitleaks Pre-Commit as the First Wall
text.summary: Gitleaks runs as a `pre-commit` hook on every staged diff; the hook is mandatory and must never be bypassed with `--no-verify`.

section.title: Local block before history
section.p: The cheapest place to stop a secret is before `git commit` writes it to local history. Once a secret hits any commit object, even a deleted commit, it must be rotated — `git filter-repo` does not undo the leak. Gitleaks runs sub-second on staged files only, so the cost is invisible.

rule.statement: Every repo MUST install `gitleaks` as a `pre-commit` hook scanning staged content. The hook MUST run by default for every contributor (humans and agents). Agents MUST treat a hook failure as a real finding: rotate first, then scrub from the diff. Bypassing with `git commit --no-verify` is forbidden — fix the root cause and recommit.
rule.rationale: Defense in depth begins where the secret would otherwise originate; CI scanners catch what slips through but cannot un-publish a token that landed on a public branch. Local-first matches the same "shift-left" pattern that made format/lint hooks effective and that GitGuardian's 2026 sprawl report identifies as the highest-leverage point.

example.code: - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks rev: v8.21.0 hooks: - id: gitleaks
example.note: Pinned by tag; runs on staged files only.
Bypass turns the hook into theatre; the next CI scan or, worse, an attacker, finds the secret.

antipattern.description: Adding `*.env`, `*.pem`, `credentials.json` to `.gitignore` and considering the problem solved.
antipattern.reason: `.gitignore` only protects untracked files; once a secret is committed it stays in history regardless. Combine `.gitignore` with the hook plus a gitleaks allowlist.

section.reference: Allowlist tuned to skip docs and fixtures.
`pre-commit` framework block wiring the hook in.
